region_of_origin: Vietnam / Singapore (Vietnamese-origin dish served in Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hu Tieu Tom or Vietnamese prawn noodle soup. FOODON_00002700 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soup) is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for Vietnamese-style prawn noodle soup (Hu Tieu Tom) in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Hu Tieu Tom or Vietnamese prawn noodle soup. SNOMED CT does not have sufficient granularity for this dish.

allergens: gluten (possible from soy sauce or wheat-based noodle variants)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of prawn noodle soup (Hu Tieu Tom) weighing approximately 500 g, comprising rice noodles, several prawns, broth, and garnishes such as bean sprouts and spring onions.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Noodle Soup (Hu Tieu Tom) — estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central component data for rice noodle soup with seafood
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table (2020); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Hu Tieu Tom exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by combining FAO/INFOODS SEA data for rice noodle soup with seafood (closest analogous dish) and USDA SR Legacy component data for prawns, rice noodles, and pork-based broth. Per-100g values reflect a typical prepared bowl composition (approximately 35% noodles, 15% prawns, 45% broth, 5% vegetables and garnish).

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, boiled — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Hu Tieu Tom
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hu Tieu Tom. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which is the primary glycemic driver in this dish. The broth, prawns, and vegetables contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as: (53 × 42.5 g available carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 22.5, rounded to 22.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pork broth, fish sauce, and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a standard 500 g bowl may contribute approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a full bowl provides approximately 42.5 g of available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~22.6, which is moderate; reducing noodle portion and increasing prawn and vegetable content can lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from pork-based broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content (~2100 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat contribution from pork broth; opting for a smaller portion or a lighter broth variant is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Noodle Soup (Hu Tieu Tom), known in Vietnamese as Hủ Tiếu Tôm, is a Vietnamese-origin noodle soup dish widely served in Singapore, particularly at Vietnamese restaurants. The dish consists of thin, translucent rice noodles (hu tieu) submerged in a clear, savoury pork-based broth that is typically simmered with dried shrimp, garlic, and fish sauce to develop a deep umami flavour. The soup is topped with fresh or boiled prawns, bean sprouts, spring onions, fried shallots, and sometimes sliced pork or quail eggs. The texture is light and silky from the rice noodles, with a clean, mildly sweet broth and a satisfying bite from the prawns. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~68 kcal per 100 g), relatively high in protein from the prawns (~5.2 g per 100 g), and low in fat (~1.8 g per 100 g), making it a lighter noodle soup option compared to richer coconut-milk or curry-based soups. However, the sodium content is high (~420 mg per 100 g, or approximately 2100 mg per standard 500 g bowl) due to the fish sauce and pork broth, which is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the rice noodles is approximately 53 (low), but the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~22.6), requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and gluten-free in its traditional rice-noodle form, though cross-contamination or soy sauce additions may introduce gluten. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the pork broth and seafood components.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Hu Tieu Tom exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA analogous rice noodle soup with seafood data and USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice noodles. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and based on approved sources, but direct measurement data for this specific dish is not available.
notes: 1. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry was found for Hu Tieu Tom. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy source data for this dish type. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish type. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish type. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in rice noodle and seafood-based soups per USDA SR Legacy data. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Hu Tieu Tom was found. 7. Halal status is listed as false because the dish traditionally contains pork broth; halal-certified variants may exist at specific restaurants. 8. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant (Circular Road, Singapore) per the GrabFood source record. 9. Cuisine is classified as Singapore (Vietnamese-origin dish served in Singapore context).
